slides.title: Cross-Lingual Automated Short-Answer Scoring
slides.subtitle: Fairness-Aware ESAS for Mandarin-Medium Secondary Education
slides.author: ZI-YUE,CHAO

left.title: The Problem
content: English ESAS systems (QWK~0.85) cannot transfer to zh-TW without significant bias
Discourse markers, sentence-length norms, and rubric anchors differ structurally across languages
Deploying English models on zh-TW responses introduces systematic DIF at score boundaries

right.title: Our Approach
content: Phase 1: GPT-4 zero-shot baseline (QWK=0.674) — establishes upper-bound without fine-tuning
Phase 2: BERT fine-tuned per-prompt + rubric prefix (QWK=0.836)
Phase 3: Cross-lingual evaluation — ZH-BERT-FT achieves QWK=0.811 with 0 DIF flags

slides.title: Cross-Lingual QWK — EN vs zh-TW by Model Strategy
slides.note: Prompts P1–P4 · test set · error bars = σ across 3–5 seeds

slides.title: Model Comparison — Key Metrics
slides.note: QWK = Quadratic Weighted Kappa · DIF = Differential Item Functioning (Mantel-Haenszel, p<.05)

slides.title: Conclusion & Next Steps
conclusions: Language-matched fine-tuning (ZH-BERT-FT) achieves QWK=0.811 on zh-TW — parity with English, zero DIF bias
Zero-shot cross-lingual transfer is both inaccurate (QWK=0.541) and systematically unfair (6/12 score levels flagged)
Rubric-prefix conditioning is the single highest-value intervention (+2.2pp) for argumentation-heavy prompts
next_steps: Complete P5–P8 zh-TW annotation (budget approval pending; target: 2026-06-10)
Contrastive-pair augmentation for P2/P7 score-boundary confusion
Begin paper draft: fairness-aware cross-lingual ESAS framework
